title: Spinning Rambouillet wool
subtitle: A fine, long-stapled merino-type fleece that spins smoothly on wheel or spindle
excerpt: Rambouillet is the closest American equivalent to Merino: a fine, dense, slightly longer-stapled fleece with a natural crimp that builds twist readily. It spins a smooth, even yarn from DK to lace weight and is softer next to the skin than many British breeds.
sourceNotes: Rambouillet breed characteristics documented in commercial fibre preparation literature and spinning guild references. The breed is descended from French Rambouillet sheep developed from Spanish Merino stock in the 18th century.

glossaryTerms.definition: The length of an individual lock of fleece when fully extended. Rambouillet staple length runs 6 to 9 cm, longer than standard merino and easier to draft on a wheel.
The natural wave structure within a wool fibre. Rambouillet has a fine, regular crimp that adds elasticity to the spun yarn and builds twist quickly.
A spinning technique in which the drafting zone is kept very short and the fibres remain parallel. Produces a smooth, firm yarn; suited to the well-aligned fibres of Rambouillet tops.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. Tie hair back before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Spinning wheel (a low-ratio drive setting for fine singles)

content.text: If working from lock rather than commercially prepared tops, flick each lock open with a flicker brush before spinning. Rambouillet locks are dense and slightly greasy even when processed; flicking opens the tip of each lock and removes the worst vegetable matter before it goes through the wheel.

Set the wheel to a moderate-to-high twist ratio: Rambouillet builds twist readily but the fine fibres need enough twist to hold a stable singles. A drive ratio of 10:1 to 14:1 works well for DK to sport weight. Spin Z-twist singles, keeping the drafting zone short for smooth tops. The yarn will be noticeably finer than a Shetland or BFL spun on the same setting because of Rambouillet's fine fibre diameter.

Ply two Z-spun singles together with S-twist to produce a balanced 2-ply. Wind onto the niddy-noddy and soak the skein in warm water for 15 minutes to set the twist. Snap the skein gently between your hands while damp to align the fibres, then hang with a light weight to dry. Rambouillet recovers its elasticity fully once the skein is dry.

items.problem: The singles are overtwisted and kink back on themselves
items.cause: Drive ratio too high or treadling too fast relative to drafting speed
items.fix: Reduce the drive ratio by one step and slow the treadling cadence. Rambouillet builds twist very readily; it needs less twist than many coarser breeds.

items.problem: The yarn is fuzzy with loose fibre sticking out from the surface
items.cause: Working from carded roving with a long drafting zone, which randomises the fibre direction
items.fix: Switch to a shorter draft or pre-draft the roving into thinner lengths before spinning. Alternatively, use combed tops which keep fibres more parallel and produce a smoother yarn surface.
